sentences.text: Well, he did disappear and his family, his mother, his other brother, and his sister, came with us.
And we were marched to the train station, which was like all the way to the end of the city, and was [? hot ?.] And we had like three, four dresses on and the coats and everything.
And we were going, marching.
And we were carrying the packages and it was very heavy, because it was a very big distance.
With the entire family.
And my younger brother says to my mother, let me help you, Mommy.
I want to help you.
So that strike me so good, you know, that here is a little two by nothing.
He-- he-- he wasn't even four years old. '38.
No, he was six, going to be six.
So he wanted to help my mother to carry the black coffee.
The pitcher was almost as tall as he was, but he wanted to help.
So once we get to the train station, it wasn't actually the regular station.
It was where they were loading cargo, sort of like heavy things.
And we were seated around there in the ground.
And there was this girls who belong to the head of the police department, the two girls whom I used to go to gymnasium together.
She came around and she said, stretch your leg.
And she was searching if you have any gold or any valuables hidden between your leg-- that was the first time that I experienced such a search-- and under your arm.
And here she was emptying the pockets and was such a thorough clean-up job.
Then she was asking if anybody has money, they should hand that over.
So I said to her, the gendarme had taken us already, even before, at home.

Then I had seen that some of these people who we were together in the same house in the ghetto, that they were-- because in Europe, you had this matchbox that-- it was a small little box, like they had in this country, they have the Matchbox cars, the same thing.
It had matches in them it.
So they had full of $100 pengo.
And they were burning it.
It was they were walking between the people who were seated on the ground.
And I said, my god.
Here they are burning the money, but they didn't had money to pay for the milk.
So once we were pushed up on the train, 80 person with the bundles, small, big, heavy, light, had to go up into the cattle train.
When we went up to the cattle train, my father made sure that we were seated, you know, back to the train.
And they-- before they closed the door, they handed us one bucket.
One bucket.
So we didn't know that time what is the bucket for.
And no longer we were seated there, people had to relieve themself.
They had to the-- so they wanted to use the bucket.
So my father said, OK.
I have this blanket.
Two people should hold it around that until they relieve themself into the bucket.
And as the train was going, it wasn't pleasant, because the odor was circulating all over and the people was perspiring.
It was hot and everything.
As we were going, we were watching-- they were watching through the little slip on the-- on the wagon where we are passing by.
As a matter of fact, there were times when I looked through the little slit and I had seen that there was another trainload of people on the next track from us, also Jewish people.
No.
No.
That I-- I-- I never-- never showed any interest to-- to watch it.
But that is a statistic that how many Jews were taken from the Kisvarda ghetto.
If I want to find out, I could find out.
So as we were going, then my father says that I see some-- that we are passing through Czechoslovakia.
I see some Czech signs.
Then we slept the night.
And then the next day he said, I see some Polish signs.
He said where are they taking us that first we pass Czechoslovakia, now we are passing Poland?
So we were really thirsty.
We were suffering.
And what-- the meantime, what I forget to mention that when the train stopped, the door was open and the Germans were there.
And they said that if you want me to take the-- if you want me to let you take the bucket down with the extremes, then you have to give me money.
You have to give me gold.
You have to give me diamonds.
Otherwise, you don't get water and you don't get the-- the dirty bucket to be emptied.
So there were people who still had ring or something, so they gave him.
The second time was the same thing.
But the meantime, the bucket was so full that when the train was ricketing back and forth, it spilled over.
So then this was going on.
You get water for gold.
You get-- you get bucket emptied for gold or [INAUDIBLE].
So luckily, we-- we had some money.
Then, by the time we get to the-- to the end of the line, they said that if you have any walking stick or if you have any soap, you should hand it over.
When my mother heard that, she said, they're not taking us to work.
If they take us to work, we have to keep ourself clean and we need the soap.
How can they take the soap away?
And why do they say the walking stick away?
It was-- it was a very, very big uproar.
I mean, it really-- it shocked the-- the-- the people.
How much time had you been on the train by then?
We were there like almost four day-- no, four nights and three days.
And nobody gave soap, because the soap was rationed and whatever soap you had, it was in the bottom of the package.
So they were hollering and screaming, you give the soap.
You give the soap.
You give the walking stick.
And we didn't have and I said, we don't have it.
So then we were going and it came nighttime.
And we-- we seen the sign, something written out in Polish, but we didn't-- we didn't pay attention to it.
Then nighttime was.
We were sitting there in the-- in the train.
We were sitting.
The train is not going anywhere.
And then suddenly, middle of the night, the door opens with a bang and the light is coming in and the-- the striped people are coming in.
So we thought that this striped-clothes men, they are crazy, that they are from the asylum or they are-- they are criminals, because who was wearing striped clothes in Europe?
Nobody was wearing it.

So the-- these people, when they came in, those three, four mens, they started to say it in Yiddish, all the young mothers give their children to the-- to the grandmothers or to somebody-- older person.
Do not take your children with you.
Do not take your children with you.
And just take-- leave all your bundles.
Leave all your packages.
Just go, go, go down, as fast as you can.
Don't worry about the packages.
You will get it.
You will be resettled.
You will get the packages.
Just leave the small children to the older people.
So we were dazed, because we were traveling and we were squeezed up.
In the meantime, we had some dead people in the train, so they pushed it on the side and they were sitting on it, because there were no room for a stiff body to take up that much room.
So we were really like shaken up sort of.
So then when we got out of the-- the wagon and we were marching, they said that all the men go on one side and all the women go to the other side.
So my older brother Philip took my younger brother Charlie with him and he put him on his shoulder, because we didn't find one of his shoes.
As we were getting off, we didn't find one of his shoes.
So as we were walking, I see that there is a big floodlight.
And a very well-dressed German soldier is there, standing there.
And meantime, my brother came back.
And he gave my younger brother-- Philip came back and he gave my younger brother Charlie to my mother, because he said, they told me to give the children back to the mother.
And that was the last time I saw him.
Then we were marching with my mother.
And as we get to the light, front of the soldiers, he just took with the stick the-- like a cane, he made a note that my older sister Clara should go.
And then he made a note that I should go there.
And then I went-- bent over and I kissed my mother.
And my mother said, please, children, take care each other.
Make sure that you watch each other.
So as I'm turning, I see that the-- this German soldier pushed my younger sister, who was that time 13 and a 1/2, 14 years old.

And as we are standing in line and we were marching towards the light, there were people who kept on whispering that the reason they want the young ones, because they're going to make prostitute out of them.
They going to send them to the-- to the German soldiers, to the front, you know, or they will send them to the soldiers in the barracks and all that.
So when I heard that, I said, my god, my youngest sister.
So I took my sister when-- then that soldier separated, I took her back.
And I kept on saying in Hungarian that she's too young.
She's too young.
He gave us such a hit on our back that I thought my skin split open, and so is for my sister.
Then we were standing there, crying a little.
Then I didn't see my mother anymore.
I didn't see my-- my brother or my two sisters.
I didn't see anybody.
Oh, by the way, when-- when I mentioned that when the gendarme came to our house and were taking us away to the ghetto, I said my younger-- my other sister was by my grandparents.
When they took my grandparents into the ghetto in Kisvarda, she came back to us, so that's how we were all together.
And we were looking--
No.
He was a German.
He was a German.
We didn't know who he was.
As we were standing there, the mans had disappeared.
The womans disappeared.
And we are standing there and we see this tremendous big building with the chimney.
The smoke is coming, with fires in it.
And then in back of the building, I hear screaming from there.
And I see the big fire is-- is flashing up, is coming up.
So I said to one of the girl, I said, you know, they are burning up the people alive, because they are screaming like that.
So there was another-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] old maid.
She said, how could you say such a thing?
You want to make a riot?
You want us-- they should kill us?
How could you say that they are burning the-- the people alive?
I said, then you tell me what is that big flame over there and what is that screaming, I said, that it goes through your heart the way they are screaming and screaming.
In the meantime, there was one truckload of people who were taken off from the sick wagon.
And there was this very well-to-do man whose son had diphtheria.
And he was with his child in the-- in the sick wagon.
And as they were passing by, he kept on saying that the Dr.
[INAUDIBLE] send us to the-- send us to hell, send us to die.
And his voice is still in my ear, the way he was screaming that-- so he must know what-- where they are taking him.
But what happened is that we didn't see, except we just seen the light, the fire as it was coming up.
And the smell was unbearable, that-- that flesh burning smell.
So we were standing there, standing there.
It was getting light already.
Then we were taken to a place.
A German woman came, a soldier, which later on I learned that you call them [NON-ENGLISH].
And he-- she took us, schnell, schnell, schnell, schnell.
So we were taken to a place.
No.
No.
Not at all.
We thought that after we were all selected and anything, that comes the weekend, that we would-- after work, we would meet the family.
That's-- that's what we were hoping.
That's what we were [INAUDIBLE] believe.
That's what we believe.
So then when they were taking us, I looked around, and I saw that these were all young girls who were with us, who were in our group.
Then when we were taken into a place, as we were marched, we had to go through like a ditch.
And as we walked, it had a wooden plank over it.
And as I looked down, it was like white thing, like ashes in the ditch.
I said, where would all these ashes come from, you know.
I mean, is-- is-- sometimes when I think back, that how come that that thing caught my eyes as we were gong.
Then when we get to the building, as they march us in through the door, I see that the striped uniform man are sitting on a wooden-- or a-- or a earthen step.
And there is a big, big, big round machine that he's feeding the clothes into the machine.
But there was about three or four of them lined up and they were all doing it.
And the way he looked at me with his eyes, I never forget that eyes.
And I wanted to say something in Hungarian, but he didn't understand Hungarian, so I couldn't talk to him.
And that in one time, I remember that he showed-- [POINTS] and I didn't understand this either, that what does it mean.
No.
So then as we were going in, there were a lot of girls sitting.
And there was like little stools.
They told us that we should stand up on the school-- stool and stretch our leg.
So we stretched our leg and they started to shave us.
And we were so embarrassed.
I mean, here is another girl shaving us.
Then they said, bend down.
And they cut us-- cut our hair.
So we felt like-- like naked without our hair.
And they cut you at your vagina, all the hair, and even under your arm.
So then they said, OK.
You go into this room and you undress.
And make sure you put everything in one place so that you will know when you come out of the shower that that's yours.
And take the stocking out of the shoes.
So we did that.
We lined it up nicely.
We go in, and then we are waiting.
And finally the water starts to dribble.
By the time we would start to wash ourselves, the-- the water stopped.
We come out, the whole place, the foyer is empty, no clothing.
Only the shoes is there, only the shoes, nothing else.
So we picked up the shoes.
And then they said that when you go out, you will get your dress.
The whole transfer, the second transfer from Kisvarda, whom they had selected.
If I say were 200, 250, that's all.
Yeah.
Yeah.
We were together.
Then when we-- we go outside there is this woman with the long hair and the civilian clothes on her, or striped clothes.
I think she had the striped clothes on her.
And she was handing us a dress, nothing else, just one dress.
Schnell, schnell, schnell, schnell.
One knock, one knock, the other knock.
I mean, they were great about this schnell, schnell business, so that you are still in a daze.
You didn't know what-- what.
And meantime, it's daylight already.
You see that there are fences and there are like electric wires all over-- all over the place and everything.
So we are standing there.
And they start to march us.
And as we are marching, we are going through lines and lines of buildings.
I see on both sides that they have like army barracks made out of wood.
And as we are walking, I see people are standing on line, and so many people, thousands of them.
On-- on one side I see the man.
The other side I see the womans.
And all the womans on the right side, they were all in civilian clothing.
All the men on the left side, they were all wearing this striped uniform.
So we are marched and we finally get to the-- one of the gate.
And I look up and it says, Arbeit-- whatever is the other thing.
I never was good in German.
So what happened is we go in and they leave us there, back of the gate.
They open the gate where the German guard is guarding it and we go in.
And we see that on both side-- no.
On one side, on the-- on the right side-- when you walk in on the right side, that there is like a small brick building.
And a lot of girls are standing on line front of it.

Meantime, I see that on the other side, where the mans were, that I hear the music and they are playing the music, the most beautiful music just like the-- the German soldiers who were selecting us at [INAUDIBLE], he was singing 'The Merry Widow' and he was swinging his bat.
Now the same thing.
There was a group of people there who were in a-- in a stage sort of things.
And they were wearing black tuxedos and they were-- they were playing all different beautiful music.
And the people started to march, all the men with the little belt on them and the bucket attached to it.
And they were all marching, marching, marching.
It was no end to it.
Then I seen that there were this tall, beautiful Gypsy womans walking.
And they had-- on their head they had like these woven baskets.
And they were walking.
Then on the right, there was a barrack.
And I see that very well-dressed, very heavy rouged girls were coming, going, laughing, and they were-- I said to my sister, we are in a-- in a crazy place.
I mean, I've never seen such a things like this.
And we are standing there.
We are standing.
And a certain group was taken away from there.
Meantime, the big heavy rain with their like-- like-- like ice in it.
It was very cold, very bad, and very big drops.
And we are standing there.
And the rain is coming on our freshly shaven head, that you don't know what to do, because you feel very miserable, very, very cold.
So we didn't know what do, so we bend down, we picked up our dress, and we covered our head.
We figured, let them see our behind, but-- but our head, we want to cover it up.
Then we couldn't stand it any longer.
The sun was coming out.
So I said to one of the girl who spoke German, I said, why don't one person go over to this German guard and say that this is too much already?
I mean, we can't take it anymore, that he should shot us and finish them once and for all.
So I said, do you all agree on it?
I mean, this is ridiculous.
So they said, OK.
Let one girl go over.
And they said we want to be shot and that's it.
We want to be finished.
So she goes over and she says to the German soldier what we want.
A few minutes later, a German woman comes to the gate, not inside, in the gate.
And she is very furious with us, very angry.
And she said in German, what do you people think, that you tell us what we should do?
Did you ever see we shot anybody?
Do you know anybody who was shot by us?
We don't shoot anybody.
We don't shoot anybody.
So we kept quiet.
And we were standing there, standing there.
And towards end of the day almost, they finally marched us into-- into one of the barrack.
When we go in, that barrack had no bed, no-- no furniture, nothing except the bare floor.
And from the rain, it was like mud all over the place.
So I said to my sister, how can we sleep in here?
I mean there's no straw, no blanket, nothing.
And I said, if we're going to lay down on this mud, we're going to get cholera, because this is ridiculous.
So we were there.
And finally, we were standing [NON-ENGLISH].
They lined us up five in a-- at that time, I didn't know what was [NON-ENGLISH], five in a row.
And they were giving some kind of a soup.
And some-- it looked like a-- a gray earthen brick, you know?
They make the brick from earth.
So we are waiting.
Then we get nothing.
So my sister was so thirsty and-- and so tired, she says to one of the woman, please, have mercy.
Give me a-- a [INAUDIBLE], because meantime, five people were drinking from one little pot.
So one of them said, here, have a taste.
So when my sister had a swallow, she said, my god, she said, what did you give me?
This is worse than the dishwashing water.
So she spit it out.
So the woman said, you graceful this and this, you know.
How can you be like that?
Here I'm sharing it with you and you spit it out?
So then we'd seen that they all get the bread, that gray things.
We get nothing.
So we had to ask, how come we are getting nothing?
So they said, because you weren't registered and we didn't get any portion for you.
So this was the introduction to Auschwitz.
Then next morning, as we are standing out-- in the meantime, we were standing against the-- the side of the barrack.
And that's the way we were sleeping standing up, because by that time we were so exhausted that we were sleeping standing up.
We went out next morning, standing on [INAUDIBLE].
And one of this beautiful woman with red, long hair, with white skin like cream, she start to speak in German.
She said, you lousy this and this and this and this and this.
You see that chimney over there.
That's where your mother and your family been through, right through that-- the smoke.
The smoke you see, that's them.
They are just coming got.
You came through the gate on your feet, but you're going to go out that way.
I looked at my sister and I said, that's a nice welcome, that this is a real crazy place.
So then what happened with-- as time was going by, I-- I got to look around and I see this one, I see that one.
Then a few days later, I had met my cousin who I used to go to school together from Kisvarda, because they came with the first transport to-- to this place.
Then I find out the name of the place was Auschwitz, Birkenau.
So when I met her, we hugged and we kissed.
And I said to her, whom are you with?
I mean, we are here and we would like you should come with us.
She said, oh, no.
She said, I'm staying with my friends and we are together.
So I said to her, you are foolish, because we are your blood relative.
I mean, why-- why do you stay with your friends?
No, I want to stay with my friend.
Meantime, we were getting this soup which consisted of grass that they had cut off from the side of the street or side of the road.
It was sometimes nails in it.
Sometimes it was all kinds of dirt in it.
And what we liked the most, it had like-- you know when you make the flour with fat if you want to thicken up a soup or something.
So we thought that the white lumps what we found in the soup, that that's that.
So we were eating and we were in seven heaven.
Later on, we found out that white things in the-- in the-- in the soup and in the coffee, that was brom, that that's the brom that stopped our period.
From the time we arrived to Auschwitz, we never had a period until way after we went home, we were liberated.
But that time, we didn't know that.
And every day they used to come to select.
First they came.
They made us to form a round circle.
And they were collecting all the pregnant ones.

As a matter of fact, at one occasion they had selected my sister Clara, because from the brom, she had blown up because every day she had such a heartburn that she would feel like to take her inside out and just shake it and air it out.
That's how bad her-- her heartburn was.
So what happened, they selected her.
And she was screaming and crying that I've never even slept with a man.
How can I be pregnant?
I'm not a-- a whore.
I'm a-- So this Lola who greeted us that you're going to go through the chimney-- seems to me, even though she was Polish, that she did understand Hungarian, because she picked up my sister.
At that time, they were selecting them and they had no clothes on them.
They were naked.
So she pushed her through the back door of the barrack and she threw a dress after her.
Meantime, I have to tell you, my dear Ellen, that we did not recognize each other after we got the clothes when they took away our own clothing.
OK.
